patches_submitted.subject: [PATCH v2 1/9] memcg: convert task->objcg to a per-node objcgs array

version_history.subject: [PATCH v2 1/9] memcg: convert task->objcg to a per-node objcgs array

series_items.subject: [PATCH v2 2/9] memcg: charge kmem pages and slab objects against per-node objcg
series_items.patch_summary: This patch updates the way memory cgroup (memcg) charges kmem pages and slab objects against per-node object cgroups (objcgs). It introduces a new helper function __current_obj_cgroup(int nid) that allows callers to specify the target node for the objcg lookup. The patch then uses this helper in two places: __memcg_kmem_charge_page() and __memcg_slab_post_alloc_hook(). This change ensures that kmem pages and slab objects are charged against the correct per-node objcg, even when the allocating CPU's node differs from the allocation's node.

series_items.subject: [PATCH v2 3/9] mm: percpu: fix obj_exts metadata charge size
series_items.patch_summary: This patch fixes a metadata charge size calculation error in the Linux kernel's percpu code. The function pcpu_obj_full_size() was using an incorrect size for the obj_exts metadata, which is a vector of struct pcpuobj_ext. The fix involves changing the size calculation to use sizeof(struct pcpuobj_ext) instead of sizeof(struct obj_cgroup *). This patch addresses a bug introduced in commit 8f30d2660a38 and is part of a larger series of patches.

series_items.subject: [PATCH v2 4/9] mm: percpu: Split memcg charging and kmem accounting
series_items.patch_summary: This patch splits memory group (memcg) charging and kernel memory (kmem) accounting into two separate steps to handle per-memcg-per-node kmem accounting. The first step, obj_cgroup_precharge(), reserves pages for memcg limit enforcement without updating kmem stats. The second step, obj_cgroup_account_kmem(), accounts kmem after the allocation succeeds and places any remaining sub-page remainder in the object stock. This decoupling allows temporary overcharging of memcg but ensures that things return to normal through obj_stock draining.

series_items.subject: [PATCH v2 5/9] mm: memcontrol: track MEMCG_KMEM per NUMA node
series_items.patch_summary: This patch introduces per-node obj_cgroup objects to track MEMCG_KMEM statistics for NUMA nodes. It removes the MEMCG_KMEM field and wires generic functions to use the new per-node accounting. The patch does not convert kmem users to proper per-memcg-per-node accounting, which is done in subsequent patches. This change aims to improve memory management and tracking on systems with multiple NUMA nodes.

series_items.subject: [PATCH v2 6/9] mm: percpu: per-node kmem accounting
series_items.patch_summary: This patch introduces per-node kmem accounting for percpu allocations. It decouples the memcg charging from kmem accounting, allowing the post-alloc hook to account pages on their actual node rather than just the best-effort placement node. The patch also optimizes the accounting process by batching per node instead of per page, reducing the number of memcg work operations proportional to the number of nodes. Additionally, it precharges enough pages to cover the worst-case scenario where the allocation spans all nodes, ensuring that the per-node credit never runs short.

series_items.subject: [PATCH v2 7/9] mm: percpu: per-node kmem accounting for obj_exts metadata
series_items.patch_summary: This patch adds per-node kmem accounting for obj_exts metadata in the Linux kernel's mm/percpu.c file. The obj_exts array is vmalloc'd and its pages may reside on different nodes, so it walks these pages to add each page's slice to the correct node's byte accumulation. This ensures that each node is rounded up to whole pages only once, across payload and metadata combined.

series_items.subject: [PATCH v2 8/9] mm: percpu: skip the per-cpu node walk on single-node systems
series_items.patch_summary: This patch optimizes the per-cpu memory allocation by skipping a time-consuming walk of possible CPU nodes on single-node systems. The existing code would perform this walk to determine the NUMA node for each backing page, but since all pages are already on the same node in a single-node system, this step is unnecessary and only adds overhead. A new fast path has been added to pcpu_memcg_accumulate() that directly attributes the allocation to the first online node when there's only one online node, thus avoiding the O(num_possible_cpus) walk.

series_items.subject: [PATCH v2 9/9] mm: zswap: per-node kmem accounting for zswap/zsmalloc
series_items.patch_summary: This patch updates zswap and zsmalloc to use per-node object cgroup (obj_cgroup) for kernel memory (kmem) accounting, attributing compressed page charges to the correct NUMA node. However, it does not correctly handle entries that straddle pages across multiple nodes, which will be addressed in a separate series by Joshua Hahn. The patch adds a function `zs_handle_to_nid` to determine the NUMA node for a given zsmalloc handle and updates the `zswap_store_page` function to use this information. This change is necessary to ensure accurate kmem accounting for compressed pages.

review_comments.author: Usama Arif
review_comments.summary: Reviewer Usama Arif noted that zs_handle_to_nid() does not acquire pool->lock like other handle-to-zspage paths, potentially causing a race condition between zswap's uncharge path and zspage migration.
review_comments.raw_body:
Does this need the same locking as the other handle-to-zspage paths?
zs_free() takes pool->lock before handle_to_obj() because zspage migration can
update or move the object behind the handle. This helper does the same decode
without the lock, so zswap's uncharge path can race migration and charge or
uncharge the wrong node, or observe transient zspage state.

review_comments.author: Nhat Pham
review_comments.summary: Reviewer Nhat Pham suggested charging compressed page charges to the page's node for now, and then revisiting the correct attribution once Joshua's patch series is in.
review_comments.raw_body:
Can we just charge it to the page's node for now? Once Joshua's patch
series is in, we can correctly charge the node owning the data :)

FWIW, this is how these zswap entries are organized in the LRU too -
following to the OG page's node.

patches_submitted.patch_summary: This patch converts the task_struct's objcg field to a per-node array of obj_cgroup pointers, allowing for efficient and accurate accounting of memory usage across multiple NUMA nodes. The change addresses issues with current_obj_cgroup() returning an objcg whose nid did not match the current CPU's node, defeating per-node vmstat batching and accounting hierarchy. The patch also introduces a new tagged pointer to store the array, ensuring atomic updates and preserving existing race-with-kmem_attach semantics.

patches_submitted.subject: [PATCH v2 0/9] per-memcg-per-node kmem accounting

series_items.subject: [RFC PATCH v2 1/9] memcg: convert task->objcg to a per-node objcgs array
series_items.patch_summary: This patch converts the task_struct's objcg field to a per-node array of obj_cgroup pointers, allowing for more efficient and accurate memory accounting across multiple NUMA nodes. The current_obj_cgroup() function is updated to index this array by numa_node_id(), and the current_objcg_update() function refreshes all entries in the array under an RCU read lock. The patch also introduces a new tagged pointer format for the obj_cgroup array, which preserves existing semantics with kmem_attach().

series_items.subject: [RFC PATCH v2 2/9] memcg: charge kmem pages and slab objects against per-node objcg
series_items.patch_summary: This patch updates the way memory cgroup (memcg) charges kmem pages and slab objects against per-node object cgroups (objcgs). It introduces a new helper function __current_obj_cgroup() that takes a node ID as an argument, allowing for more accurate charging of memory against the correct node's objcg. The patch also updates callers in the kmem accounting path to use this new helper function, ensuring that per-node charge attribution and vmstat batching are correctly routed to the intended sibling of the same memcg. This change addresses a problem where allocations on one node were being charged against an objcg whose nid did not match the allocation's physical node, leading to incorrect charging and attribution.

series_items.subject: [RFC PATCH v2 3/9] mm: percpu: fix obj_exts metadata charge size
series_items.patch_summary: This patch fixes a metadata charge size calculation error in the Linux kernel's percpu code. The function pcpu_obj_full_size was using the wrong size for the obj_exts metadata, which is actually an array of struct pcpuobj_ext rather than a single struct obj_cgroup pointer. The fix involves changing the size calculation to use sizeof(struct pcpuobj_ext) instead of sizeof(struct obj_cgroup *). This patch addresses a problem introduced by a previous commit that added support for profiling memory allocations.

series_items.subject: [RFC PATCH v2 4/9] mm: percpu: Split memcg charging and kmem accounting
series_items.patch_summary: This patch splits memory group (memcg) charging and kernel memory (kmem) accounting into two separate steps to handle per-memcg-per-node kmem accounting. It introduces new functions `obj_cgroup_precharge` and `obj_cgroup_account_kmem` to decouple the page counter charge from KMEM stats, allowing for temporary overcharging of memcgs during allocation. The patch also updates existing functions in `mm/memcontrol.c` and `include/linux/memcontrol.h` to reflect this change.

patches_submitted.patch_summary: This patch series aims to improve kmem accounting by introducing per-memcg-per-node accounting, which reduces the overhead of discovering each backing page's real node in a many-CPU system. The patches batch memcg accounting and provide alternative versions for different scenarios, including one that caches each backing page's node in the chunk.

review_comments.author: Alexandre Ghiti
review_comments.summary: The author posted an initial patch series with performance data showing significant improvements over previous versions. They also mentioned an alternative version that caches each backing page's node in the chunk, but decided not to include it in this series.
review_comments.raw_body:
Sorry for the noise, too many emails at once, I'll add some delay.

Sorry again,

Alex

On 6/26/26 12:09, Alexandre Ghiti wrote:
> This is version 2 of per-memcg-per-node kmem accounting.
>
> As asked by Joshua, I ran some microbenchmarks to check the impact of
> this fine grain accounting.
>
> TL;DR: There is a substantial impact (up to +337% on small percpu allocations)
> on a benchmark that loops over small percpu allocations. On the other hand,
> on a userspace program that creates a bpf percpu map, this cost is not visible.
>
> I followed Joshua's advice and now this version batches the memcg accounting:
> it improves the performance +337% vs +417% (v1) on 176 cores single node
> machine and +153% vs 206% (v1) on 80 cores 2 nodes machine.
>
> We can see that the overhead of this version scales linearly with the number of
> cpus (the number of nodes being small). This overhead comes mainly from
> vmalloc_to_page() so I have another variant (b) that decreases the impact even
> more (+131% vs +337% on 176 cores and +86% vs +153% on 80 cores) but I'm not
> sure the added complexity is needed so I did not send this version, let me know
> what you think.
>
> Performance
> ===========
>
> All benchmarks run in a memcg with __GFP_ACCOUNT.
>
> 1) BPF percpu map create/destroy, full series vs baseline kernel (two
>     boots, 176-CPU AMD EPYC, 1 NUMA node): the per-node accounting is lost
>     in the BPF syscall overhead, the delta is within noise (us/op):
>
>       size (B):    64     256    1024   4096   8192
>       delta:     -5.5%  -5.1%  -1.8%  -5.1%  -4.1%
>
> 2) In-kernel microbench that isolates the accounting cost: a tight
>     __alloc_percpu_gfp()/free_percpu() loop, __GFP_ACCOUNT on vs off on the
>     same boot (ACCT COST = on - off). The dominant cost on a many-CPU box
>     is discovering each backing page's real node (vmalloc_to_page() per
>     possible CPU). ACCT COST by value size:
>
>     176-CPU EPYC, 1 node
>       size (B):              64       256      1024     4096     8192
>        baseline (upstream)  +5.3%    +5.4%    +0.1%    -1.8%    -0.5%
>        v1 credit (per-page) +417.3%  +182.5%  +68.5%   +21.4%   +16.1%
>     a) per-node accounting  +337.8%  +141.8%  +36.1%   +11.9%   +6.8%
>     b) per-page nid cache   +131.3%  +53.7%   +10.5%   +0.9%    +2.0%
>     c) single-node fast     +12.6%   +12.1%   +3.5%    +6.6%    +0.7%
>
>     80-CPU Xeon Gold 6138, 2 nodes (fast path inactive)
>       size (B):              64       256      1024     4096     8192
>        baseline (upstream)  +1.2%    -3.8%    +12.4%   +1.2%    +0.5%   (noise)
>        v1 credit (per-page) +206.1%  +134.0%  +44.5%   +11.6%   +11.5%
>     a) per-node accounting  +153.2%  +64.7%   +19.4%   +4.2%    +5.9%
>     b) per-page nid cache   +86.5%   +45.5%   +14.7%   +1.8%    +1.6%
>
>     (a) this patchset without fast path for single node
>     (b) is an alternative version, not in this series, that caches each backing
>         page's node in the chunk so the walk is paid once per page instead of
>         once per allocation
>     (c) this patchset with fast path for single node
>
> Changes in v2
> =============
>
> - objcg lifetime: Shakeel's patch 1 now guarantees the lifetime of every
>    per-node objcg
> - dropped patch 5 and 6 since Shakeel's patch 2 replaces them
> - fixed the number of precharged pages (the v1 formula under-precharged)
> - per-node batching (Joshua's suggestion): accumulate the per-node bytes
>    first, then issue one account_kmem()/uncharge() per touched node =>
>    O(nodes) memcg ops instead of O(num_possible_cpus)
> - single-node fast path: skip the per-cpu node walk on single node machines
> - obj_exts metadata is now accounted per-node (walk its vmalloc pages)
>    rather than charged whole to one memcg (Shakeel's main v1 objection).
> - renamed obj_cgroup_get_nid() -> obj_cgroup_nid() (returns a borrowed RCU
>    pointer, no ref taken).
> - zswap: fixed the missing locking around the per-node objcg lookup (now
>    done under RCU + obj_cgroup_tryget()).
>
> This series pursues the work initiated by Joshua [1]. We need kernel
> memory to be accounted on a per-node basis in order to be able to know
> the memcg <-> physical memory association.
>
> This series takes advantage of the recently introduced per-node
> obj_cgroup and makes those obj_cgroup tied to their NUMA node.
>
> The bulk of the series is percpu per-node accounting: percpu
> "precharges" the memcg before we know the actual location of the pages
> it uses, so charging and accounting had to be split. All other kmem
> users (slab, __memcg_kmem_charge_page) are now handled directly by
> Shakeel's per-node obj_cgroup infrastructure this series sits on, so
> only percpu and zswap need explicit per-node work here (zswap support
> is limited because Joshua is working on it in parallel [3]).
>
> Thanks Joshua and Shakeel for the early feedback!
>
> [1] https://lore.kernel.org/linux-mm/20260404033844.1892595-1-joshua.hahnjy@gmail.com/
> [2] https://lore.kernel.org/linux-mm/56c04b1c5d54f75ccdc12896df6c1ca35403ecc3.1772711148.git.zhengqi.arch@bytedance.com/
> [3] https://lore.kernel.org/linux-mm/20260311195153.4013476-1-joshua.hahnjy@gmail.com/
>
> Functional Testing
> ==================
>
> - Tested with a percpu kmem self-test in an 8-node VM (2 nodes with CPUs,
>    6 memory-only). For each allocation it checks that every node is charged
>    and later uncharged the same number of bytes -- including a CPU-less node
>    that ends up holding the obj_exts metadata -- and that nothing is left
>    charged after teardown. All checks pass. (The self-test module is not
>    part of this series.)
>
> Alexandre Ghiti (7):
>    mm: percpu: fix obj_exts metadata charge size
>    mm: percpu: Split memcg charging and kmem accounting
>    mm: memcontrol: track MEMCG_KMEM per NUMA node
>    mm: percpu: per-node kmem accounting
>    mm: percpu: per-node kmem accounting for obj_exts metadata
>    mm: percpu: skip the per-cpu node walk on single-node systems
>    mm: zswap: per-node kmem accounting for zswap/zsmalloc
>
> Shakeel Butt (2):
>    memcg: convert task->objcg to a per-node objcgs array
>    memcg: charge kmem pages and slab objects against per-node objcg
>
>   include/linux/memcontrol.h |  23 ++-
>   include/linux/mmzone.h     |   1 +
>   include/linux/sched.h      |   7 +-
>   include/linux/zsmalloc.h   |   2 +
>   mm/memcontrol.c            | 286 ++++++++++++++++++++++++++-----------
>   mm/percpu-internal.h       |   2 +-
>   mm/percpu.c                | 108 +++++++++++++-
>   mm/vmstat.c                |   1 +
>   mm/zsmalloc.c              |  11 ++
>   mm/zswap.c                 |  19 ++-
>   10 files changed, 361 insertions(+), 99 deletions(-)
>
